coredata.dc:title: Non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide on highly amidized graphene quantum dot electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Siyong
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Chandra Mallick, Bikash
Fu, Chun-Chieh
Juang, Ruey-Shin
Ashraf Gandomi, Yasser
Kihm, Kenneth D.
coredata.dc:description: An efficient infrared (IR)-assisted technique is developed to synthesize highly amidized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a metal-free catalyst for electrochemically detecting hydrogen peroxide. Through the IR-assisted pyrolysis of urea and citric acid at different chemical ratios, the GQDs with very high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23–46 at.%) are synthesized, composed of pyrrolic/pyridinic N, graphitic N, and N-oxide functionalities. Through various electrochemical diagnostics, it is confirmed that highly amidized GQD electrodes enable high catalytic activity toward H2O2 reduction. The catalytic cycle includes the adsorption of H2O2 and two-stage charge transfer steps on highly amidized GQD catalyst, where the substitutional N atoms (i.e., pyridinic N) at the edge of carbon network provide additional electroactive sites for adsorbing H2O2. The amperometric investigation followed by a rigorous linear regression analysis confirms a high selectivity of 1.83 μA mM−1 cm−2 toward H2O2 detection within the concentration range of 0.5–40 mM. The major attributes of the GQD catalytic electrodes include high sensitivity, wide detection range, fast response time, and superior selectivity. Accordingly, the robust design of GQDs developed in this work paves the way for engineering highly selective catalyst as a robust electrochemical sensor for non-enzymatic H2O2 detection at ultra-low concentrations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen peroxide sensor
Metal-free catalysts
Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doping
Electrochemical detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physiochemical and optical properties of functionalized GQDs
Electrochemical detection of H2O2 on the functionalized GQD electrodes
CV profiles of functionalized GQD electrodes
Amperometric detection of H2O2 by GQD electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra, (b) survey-scan XPS spectra, and (c) distribution of N-functionalities of different GQD samples, and (d) FL spectra of different of GQD suspensions at 360 nm. The inset of Fig. 1(d) shows the pictures of GQD suspensions under UV illumination at 360 nm.
FE-SEM image of different GQD/CP electrodes: (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD. The inset photos of Fig. 2(a), 2(b), and 2(c) shows their corresponding HR-TEM micrograph images.
Typical CV profiles of different GQD/GCE catalyst electrodes in 1 M H2O2: (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD at various scan rates of 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, and 210 mV s−1.
Schematic diagram to illustrate the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 on N-doped GQD catalysts.

Current response as a function of injection period of H2O2 at −0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl under N2 atmosphere: (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD electrodes. Linear calibration curves of current response versus H2O2 concentration for (d) LN-GQD, (e) MN-GQD, and (f) HN-GQD electrodes at −0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
Selectivity test of (a) LN-GQD, (b) MN-GQD, and (c) HN-GQD electrodes by injecting CA, GL, urea, and H2O2.

head.ce:title: Non-enzymatic electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide on highly amidized graphene quantum dot electrodes

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Mechanical, Aerospace, and Biomedical Engineering, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, TN 37996, United States.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient infrared (IR)-assisted technique is developed to synthesize highly amidized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a metal-free catalyst for electrochemically detecting hydrogen peroxide. Through the IR-assisted pyrolysis of urea and citric acid at different chemical ratios, the GQDs with very high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23–46 at.%) are synthesized, composed of pyrrolic/pyridinic N, graphitic N, and N-oxide functionalities. Through various electrochemical diagnostics, it is confirmed that highly amidized GQD electrodes enable high catalytic activity toward H2O2 reduction. The catalytic cycle includes the adsorption of H2O2 and two-stage charge transfer steps on highly amidized GQD catalyst, where the substitutional N atoms (i.e., pyridinic N) at the edge of carbon network provide additional electroactive sites for adsorbing H2O2. The amperometric investigation followed by a rigorous linear regression analysis confirms a high selectivity of 1.83 μA mM−1 cm−2 toward H2O2 detection within the concentration range of 0.5–40 mM. The major attributes of the GQD catalytic electrodes include high sensitivity, wide detection range, fast response time, and superior selectivity. Accordingly, the robust design of GQDs developed in this work paves the way for engineering highly selective catalyst as a robust electrochemical sensor for non-enzymatic H2O2 detection at ultra-low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been widely used in a broad variety of application areas including food industry, chemical synthesis, propulsion, biotechnology, pharmaceuticals, and electrochemical energy storage/conversion devices (e.g. fuel cells) [1–3]. Hydrogen peroxide is also tightly related to cell growth and signal transduction. It is recognized as a biomarker in living cells for cancer diagnostics since its concentration level is considered a critical physiological parameter for the disease research [4–6]. H2O2 used to be identified as a toxic byproduct of the aerobic metabolism, something that cells had to remove after its first identification in a living cell [7]. If H2O2 detoxification catalyzed by catalases and peroxidases was not adequate, H2O2 would diffuse and oxidize biological targets causing cellular malfunctions responsible for several pathologies and aging [8].

For these critical applications, developing a highly sensitive yet selective sensor for detecting ultra-low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide with high durability and reliability is of significant importance. Extensive efforts have been devoted to developing analytical techniques for measuring bio-specie concentrations including photo acoustic spectroscopy [9,10], capacitive detection [11], electrochemical analysis [12–14], spectrophotometry [15,16], fluorescence [17,18], and many others. Among these methods, the electrochemical techniques have received considerable attention since these techniques are usually inexpensive, easy to use, highly sensitive, and selective with a small footprint [19–21]. Two different classes of electrochemical sensors have been developed for the H2O2 detection including: (i) enzymatic and (ii) non-enzymatic. For the enzymatic H2O2 sensor, a typical peroxidase (e.g. catalase) serves as an efficient biocatalyst to increase the rate of reaction via reducing the activation energy of the chemical reaction, and subsequently catalyzing and decomposing the hydrogen peroxide into oxygen and water [22]. However, the practical applications of enzyme-based H2O2 sensors are usually limited since they have complicated preparation procedures, are costly, and degrade easily [4,22,23]. As a result, developing highly sensitive non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for H2O2 detection is needed. Designing novel electrode materials with high sensitivity and superior selectivity is of the utmost importance to the development of non-enzymatic H2O2 sensors based on H2O2 electro-oxidation and electro-reduction.

Several attempts have been made to adopt Ni-metal organic framework material-carbon [3], graphene nanohybrid [4], platinum/ZnFe2O4 functionalized reduced graphene oxide [22], NiCo2S4 functionalized reduced graphene oxide [23], and Cr (III)-organic framework [24] as the electrode materials for the H2O2 detection. Recently, highly amidized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with O-rich functionalities were successfully synthesized through efficient induction synthesis methods [25–27]. The highly amidized GQDs contain high amidation level (N/C ratio: 22–50 at.%), originated from the presence of (i) chemical N atoms such as bonding to O atoms (oxidized nitrogen species) and (ii) lattice N atoms including six-membered rings (pyridinic N), five-membered rings (pyrrolic N), and graphene basal plane (graphitic/quaternary-N center) [29–32]. With fractional carbon atoms superseded by one or more species of heteroatoms, the metal-free graphene displays remarkably superior electro-catalytic activity than undoped counterparts [4]. However, the application of N-doped GQDs as the metal-free catalyst for the hydrogen peroxide reduction has not been explored in the literature.

In this work, we have developed highly durable GQDs as the metal-free catalyst for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide. We have also explored the influence of amidation level on the catalytic activity of the GQD electrodes. Here, we have engineered highly sensitive yet selective N-doped GQDs as a platform for an easy-to-use, inexpensive and durable sensing material capable of detecting ultra-low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide.

In our previous work, we have elaborated on the synthesis route for preparing highly amidized GQD samples [28]. Following a similar approach, in this work, we have employed citric acid (CA, C6H8O7, molecular weight: 180, purity: 99%) and urea (CON2H4, molecular weight: 60, purity: 99%) as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. To explore the effect of amidation level, three types of GQD samples were prepared via adjusting different molar ratios of urea to citric acid at 1:1, 1:3, and 3:1. First, the urea and citric acid powders (total weight: 10 g) were uniformly mixed using a 2D mixer (with Zr balls) at 300 rpm for 10 min. The solid mixture was subsequently placed in an IR-heating furnace equipped with six medium-wave IR filaments operating within the wavelength of 1.4–3.2 μm. The IR-assisted pyrolysis process was carried out at 250 °C in air for 0.5 h. The GQD samples were then cooled down to the ambient temperature, moderately ball-milled, and sieved through a stainless foil mesh (Type: 200 mesh). Afterwards, all the GQD samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the samples were dehydrated at 105 °C in a vacuum oven, overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical and optical properties of functionalized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Nicolet 380 spectrometer was employed for acquiring the Fourier-transform IR (FT-IR) spectra. The morphology of highly amidized GQD samples was observed using a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU-8200) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200s). The chemical composition of the GQD samples was analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XP spectra were recorded using a Fison VG ESCA210 spectrometer equipped with Mg-Kα radiation. The XPS C 1 s and N 1 s spectra of GQD samples were deconvoluted implementing a non-linear least square optimization algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function. The fluorescence (FL) spectra of the GQD samples were also characterized, where all GQD suspensions were ultrasonically dispersed within distilled water to ensure a uniform suspension. The FL emission spectra of each suspension were acquired using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) at a fixed wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 on the functionalized GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: A three-electrode cell configuration was used for characterizing the electrochemical performance of highly amidized GQD electrodes. A catalyst-coated glassy carbon (GC) as the working electrode (WE), Pt wire as the counter electrode (CE), and Ag/AgCl reference electrode were employed within the setup. Herein, a drop-coating method was used to prepare the GQD electrodes for non-enzymatic detection toward the hydrogen peroxide present in the solution. To prepare the working electrode, 40 mL of 5 wt% Nafion solution was added to 1 mL of water/ethanol mixture (4:1 in v/v), and 10 mg of the GQD catalyst was then dispersed and treated via sonication for 10 min to obtain a homogenous ink. Followingly, 5 mL of the suspension was dropped casted onto the GC electrode (diameter of ~ 3 mm) and left to dry at the ambient temperature.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were carried out under N2 atmosphere at room temperature. The active side of the catalyst electrode was exposed to the electrolyte consisting of 0.1 M NaOH and a controlled amount of H2O2. The H2O2 concentration ranged from 0.5 to 40 mM. The electrochemical detection on the GQD electrodes was performed within the potential range of −1 to + 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl at various sweep rates of 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180, and 210 mV s−1. The amperometric (I-t) measurement at −0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl was used to examine the sensitivity toward H2O2. The I-t analysis was performed using a high-precision electrochemical analyzer (CH Instrument, Inc., CHI 608). To probe the selectivity, the sensor’s response was analyzed when other species were also present within the solution. The species including CA, glucose (GL), and urea were used for the selectivity analysis, where 1 mM of these chemicals was added into the electrolyte containing 1 mM H2O2. All the electrochemical measurements of this work were performed at the ambient temperature.

As described in the experimental section, the GQD samples were prepared with various level of amidation; here, we have labeled the samples as “LN-GQD”, “MN-GQD”, and “HN-GQD”, based on the urea/CA molar ratio used for preparing them (i.e. 1:3, 1:1, and 3:1, corresponding to “LN-GQD”, “MN-GQD”, and “HN-GQD”, respectively).

To confirm the formation of the surface functional groups on the GQD samples, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed. According to Fig. 1 (a), the transmittance band located at 1150–1300 cm−1 can be attributed to C-O stretch of –COOH originating from the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups [33–35] on GQDs. The band at ~ 1400 cm−1 corresponds to the existence of C-N group. The peak at ca. 1640 cm−1 reveals the presence of C = C stretching [36], whereas the transmittance peak taking place at ca. 1710 cm−1, mainly originates from the existence of CN or CO functional groups [28,37]. The transmittance band at ca. 3400 cm−1 is due to physically adsorbed H2O on the GQD surface. As shown in Fig. 1(a), one weak band occurring at ca. 3200 cm−1 can also be identified that reflects the N–H group attaching to the edge of carbon network.

XPS measurement was adopted to investigate the chemical compositions as well as the surface functional groups on the GQD samples. Survey-scan XPS spectra of the GQD samples are illustrated in Fig. 1(b). According to Fig. 1(b), three major peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s can be identified at ~ 284, 400, and 532 eV, respectively. The N/C atomic ratio of HN-GQD, MN-GQD, and LN-GQD samples were 46, 33, and 23 at.%, respectively. Such high magnitude of N/C atomic ratio reflects that highly amidized carbon nanostructures can be fabricated through the IR-assisted pyrolysis of urea and CA. The N/C atomic ratios of the amidized GQD samples prepared in this work are much higher than that of the carbon nanostructures prepared via other techniques including chemical vapor deposition followed by post-doping with C3N4 precursor (N content: 6.3 at.%) [38], chemical impregnation of graphene in NH3·H2O followed by post-heating at 90 °C for 12 h (N content: 5.7–6.8 at.%) [39], and hydrothermal route in the presence of NH4OH9 (N content: 12.8 at.%) [40]. It is important to note that the O/C ratio of the as-prepared samples exhibits a similar trend to that of the N/C ratio; 42, 35, and 28 at.% for HN-GQD, MN-GQD, and LN-GQD, respectively. This reveals that the GQDs also contain O-rich functionalities including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups; thus, creating a surface heterogeneity.

The distribution of N-containing groups on the GQD samples was assessed through decomposition of the N 1 s peaks at 399.6 eV (pyrrolic/pyridinic N), 400.4 eV (graphitic N), and 401.4 eV (N-oxides, e.g., O = C-N) [40,41]. Fig. 1(c) compares the distributions of N functionalities for various samples. According to Fig. 1(c), the HN-GQD sample is composed of a high fraction of graphitic N (~72.6%) resulted from the crystalline cyanuric acid (i.e., CNOH)3 embedded within the sample. This observation has also been confirmed via the X-ray diffraction analysis demonstrated in our previous study [28]. In contrast, both LN-GQD and MN-GQD samples contain high percentage of pyrrolic/pyridinic N (>50%). The MN-GQD sample also possesses high graphitic N (~44.8%) but with ultralow N-oxides level (~4.6%), as compared to the LN-GQD sample. This finding confirms that the molar ratio of urea to citric acid is a crucial factor directly affecting the amidation level as well as the N-functionalities distribution on the GQDs.

Fig. 1(d) demonstrates FL emission spectra of the GQD suspensions at 360 nm, where the inset of this figure shows the reflected wavelength of the emitted light source for the LN-GQD (blue), MN-GQD (green), and HN-GQD sample (blue + green). According to Fig. 1(d), the FL spectrum of HN-GQD sample contains twin PL peaks occurring at ca. 400 nm (3.10 eV) and 510 nm (2.43 eV), respectively. In contrast, an asymmetric peak within the range of 400 and 600 nm can be identified for both LN-GQD and MN-GQD at 360-nm excitation. The FL intensities reach their maximum levels at ca. 435 (2.85 eV) and 475 nm (2.61 eV) for LN-GQD and MN-GQD samples, respectively. Accordingly, the FL emission signature of GQD suspensions is strongly altered by the particle size, chemical composition, and surface heterogeneity.

Fig. 2 presents FE-SEM images of different GQD electrodes. As illustrated in Fig. 2, all GQDs are randomly stacked within the electrode internal structure forming a conformal nanostructured coating. The surface density of all GQD electrodes was approximated to be ~ 2.5 mg cm−2, based on the increased weight upon deposition of GQD coating per sample. The inset of Fig. 2(a)-(c) shows HR-TEM micrographs of the GQD electrodes. As clearly demonstrated in Fig. 2, the GQDs tend to form a round-like shape with an average particle size of ~ 20 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV profiles of functionalized GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammetry profiles of highly amidized GQD electrodes are depicted in Fig. 3 (a)-3(c). For conducting the CV experiments, a solution of 10 mM H2O2 + 0.1 M NaOH was used and the CV spectra were recorded at different sweep rates as illustrated in Fig. 3. As shown, the three GQD electrodes are all electrocatalytically active toward H2O2 reduction but with vastly different reductive currents as the sensor’s response upon H2O2 detection. As shown in Fig. 3, within the potential range considered for the CV experiments, the GQD electrodes demonstrate comparatively strong reduction peaks at −0.4 and −0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Considering the CV spectra at various scan rate, the kinetic parameters for the faradaic electron transfer at the electrolyte/electrode interface can be assessed for the functionalized GQDs samples. As depicted in Fig. 3, the reductive peak current demonstrates an increasing trend with increased sweep rate; thus, the reduction of H2O2 on the GQD surface is mainly controlled by the surface adsorption process [42]. This observation also reveals that highly amidized GQDs significantly enhance the electrical conductivity of the pristine carbon electrodes due to high concentration of the N atoms doped within the carbon network. As a result, the N doping is capable of inducing numerous topological defects along with improving the electron transfer kinetics. These superior attributes enabled via N doping contribute to enhancing catalytic activity and boosting the electrical conductivity of the carbon network [4]. To prove its stability, the CV profiles of functionalized GQD catalyst electrodes in sulfuric acid maintain ~ 90% charge transfer after 300 cycles as compared to their initial value at the first cycle, demonstrated by our previous study [43]. This reveals that the GQD catalysts still adhere to the carbon paper, indicating the strong adhesion by using Nafion as binder.

It is also important to formulate the mechanism of H2O2 reduction on the GQD electrodes. Further details on the mechanism of H2O2 reduction on graphene-based materials have already been reported in some previous works [44]. Here, we have proposed the following mechanism for the reduction of H2O2 on highly amidized GQD electrodes.
(R1) GQD* + H2O2 → GQD* − H2O2(ads)
(R2) GQD* − H2O2(ads) + e− → GQD* − OH(ads) + OH−
(R3) GQD* − OH(ads) + e− → GQD* + OH−
Herein the H2O2 catalytic cycle consists of (i) adsorption of H2O2 on the GQD electrodes as formulated in (R1) and (ii) two-stage single-electron charge transfer steps according to (R2) and (R3) which leads to the conformation of two OH− ions. Upon stripping GQD*−H2O2(ads) and GQD*−OH(ads) intermediates (i.e. R2 and R3), the GQD surface can be refreshed and subsequently reutilized as the active sites for the consecutive adsorption steps (i.e. R1). Accodingly, the H2O2 electro-reduction reaction can be formulated as:
(R4) H2O2 + 2 e− → 2 OH−

According to Fig. 3, we observe an onset potential at ca. −0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl with a sweep rate of 210 mV s−1 for all the GQD electrodes, whereas the MN-GQD electrode exhibits highest reductive current at a fixed rate, as compared to other samples. This reflects that MN-GQD electrode provides remarkable catalytic acitivity toward H2O2 reduction. In contrast, HN-GQD electrodes despite possessing an ultrahigh amidation level, does not provide higher reductive current compared to the MN-GQD electrode. Indeed, the HN-GQD electrodes contain highly crystalline cyanuric acid that reduces the number of active sites for adsorption of hydrogen peroxide (i.e. low active coverage).

To further clarify the mechanism of H2O2 reduciton on the N-doped GQD catalytic electrodes, a schematic diagram illustrating the multistep electron transfer route is proposed in Fig. 4 . It is already shown that the carbon atoms adjacent to the pyridinic N with Lewis basicity facsilitate the adsorption of oxygenated molecules in the initial step of reduction process (i.e. reaction (R1)) [45]. As a result, the substitutional N atoms promote the adsorption energy of H2O2 on the neighboring C-atoms serving as the active sites available for the adsorption of H2O2 molecules. Accordingly, HM-GQD catalytic electrode offers the largest number of adsorptive sites due to its high N/C atomic ratio and high fraction of pyridinic N atoms (i.e. increased active surface coverage). As a result, the adsorption of H2O2 molecules on the highly amidized GQD catalysts is energetically favored.

The subsequent charge transfer steps ((R2) and (R3)) enables the desorption of oxygenated species (H2O2(ads) and OH(ads)) with the aid of heteroatom doping. This is because the N edge-doping (i.e. pyridinic N) imparts the molecular orbital’s localized distribution due to the introduction of unpaired electrons and subsequently weakens the O − O bonding [46–48]. Moreover, the graphitic N atoms can replace the C atoms within the graphene lattice, in which the substitution maintains the sp2 hybridization of C atoms. This substitution in the graphene lattice enhances the electro-conductivity in graphene via donating the delocalized electrons [26,49–51]. Thus, the design of highly amidized GQDs promotes the adsorption of H2O2 and enhances the electron transfer kinetics, leading to a complete catalytic cycle with excellent reversibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric detection of H2O2 by GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a)-5(c) shows typical I-t response plots of highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes at −0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl in 0.1 M NaOH solution with a stepwise alteration of H2O2 concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 5, the amperometric responses for all the samples exhibit a step-like increase as a function of increased H2O2 concentration indicating high sensitivity and ultra-fast response. Therefore, the amount of injected H2O2 and the corresponding current response (i.e. H2O2 concentration versus output current) can be correlated and used as the calibration curve for the H2O2 detection, as plotted in Fig. 5(d)-5(f). According to Fig. 5, the calibration curves demonstrate excellent linearity within the concentration range of 0.5–40 mM with correlation coefficients (R 2) of LN-GQD (R 2: 0.986), MN-GQD (R 2: 0.985), and HN-GQD (R 2: 0.980). Utilizing a linear regression algorithm, the sensitivities are thus determined to be 1.54, 1.83, and 1.23 μA mM−1 cm−2 for the LN-GQD, MN-GQD, and HM-GQD catalytic electrodes, respectively. The finding confirms that the sensitivity toward H2O2 can be significantly enhanced by using highly amidized GQD catalytic electrodes with finely tuned N-rich functionalities (e.g. high fraction of pyridinic N atoms). Therefore, the GQD catalyst exhibits high sensitivity as well as short response time and is capable to operate within a wide concentration range. These unique attributes are mainly due to the synergetic effect that combines nanosized structure, high amidation level, and zero-dimensional conductive graphene, inducing superior accessibility to non-enzymatic catalysis of the hydrogen peroxide.

To probe the selectivity, the current response of the H2O2 sensor was evaluated using the chronoamperometry experiment at −0.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl for the solutions containing some contaminants. Fig. 6 demonstrates the current response of the GQD catalytic electrodes for the solution of 0.1 M NaOH when other chemicals (e.g. H2O2, CA, GL, and urea) are also present within the solution. Herein the concentration of interfering species was set at 1 mM (i.e·H2O2: interfering species = 1: 1). As shown in Fig. 6, the current response remains unchanged (i.e. the current only varies with H2O2) when the interfering species are being added to the solution confirming a superior selectivity toward H2O2. However, the current response of MN-GQD electrode shows a slight interference when adding CA. This is presumably due to the interaction between hydrophilic CA molecules and coexisting cyanuric acid/GQD interface. Thus, future work is required to purify GQDs or functionalize surface chemistry for probing some specific species, e.g., thiol group or bipolar molecules. It is important to note that even after the injection of interfering species, the sensor is still highly sensitive when the H2O2 is injected for the second time. As a result, the highly-amidized GQDs developed in this work can be considered as a metal-free and very durable catalyst for H2O2 detection with a remarkable selectivity toward hydrogen peroxide.

This work adopted an efficient IR-assisted method to synthesize highly amidized GQDs for electrochemically detecting H2O2 in the alkali electrolytes. The GQD catalysts were synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of urea and citric acid under IR irradiation at 250 °C. The GQD catalysts with an average size of 30 nm contained a high amidation level (N/C atomic ratio: 23–46 at.%), consisted of pyrrolic/pyridinic N, graphitic N, and N-oxide functionalities. Through various electrochemical diagnostics, it was confirmed that the design of highly amidized GQDs promotes the adsorption of H2O2 and facilitates the electron transfer kinetics, leading to a complete catalytic cycle (i.e. adsorption of H2O2 followed by two-stage charge transfer steps) with excellent reversibility. An injection amperometric technique was developed for probing the selectivity of GQD catalytic electrodes toward H2O2 detection. Very high selectivity of 1.83 μA mM−1 cm−2, analyzed through a linear regression plot of current response versus H2O2 concentration, was confirmed within the concentration range of 0.5–40 mM. The GQD catalytic electrodes developed in this work demonstrate remarkable performance toward H2O2 detection, including excellent sensitivity, wide detection range, fast response time, and superior selectivity. The superior catalysis efficiency of the GQD electrodes is due to the synergetic effect combining nanosized structure, high amidation level, and zero-dimensional conductive network, promoting higher accessibility to the non-enzymatic catalysis interface for the H2O2 detection. As a result, highly amidized GQDs designed in this work paves the way for developing inexpensive yet durable metal-free catalysts capable of electrochemically detecting H2O2 with high selectivity and sensitivity at ultra-low concentrations.

Siyong Gu: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation. Chien-Te Hsieh: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - original draft. Bikash Chandra Mallick: Investigation, Data curation. Chun-Chieh Fu: Data curation, Visualization. Ruey-Shin Juang: Conceptualization. Yasser Ashraf Gandomi: Validation. Kenneth D. Kihm: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the financial support the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3). Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated. Prof. Gu also thanks for the support from the Talents Introduction Program of Xiamen University of Technology (YKJ19018R) and Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in Fujian Province University (IRTSTFJ).
